item.title: Trump says several people arrested for alleged vandalism of Reflecting Pool
item.description: President Trump claims the problems with the Reflecting Pool in Washington are due to vandalism.

item.title: Company owned by Trump donor won $1.7 million no-bid Reflecting Pool contract
item.description: The federal government awarded a company owned by a Trump donor $1.7 million to install a new water cleaning system for the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool, records show.

item.title: LAUSD Superintendent Alberto Carvalho resigns amid FBI investigation
item.description: Nearly four months after federal agents raided his home and district headquarters, Superintendent Alberto Carvalho announced his resignation as the head of the Los Angeles Unified School District on Sunday.

item.title: Iran to let nuclear inspections resume as talks continue, Vance says
item.description: Vance says Iran agreed to let international nuclear inspectors back into the country during a "very, very good" first day of negotiations with the U.S.

item.title: Interstellar comet may be oldest object ever seen in solar system
item.description: Exactly where the comet 3I/ATLAS came from within the Milky Way remains a mystery.

item.title: America's long history of political turmoil
item.description: In his new book, "Been There, Done That," historian Greg Jackson shines a light on dark times in U.S. history, such as insurrections, political partisanship and disregard for the Constitution. Jackson joins "The Takeout" with more.

item.title: Jesse Welles on "This Land is Your Land" and Woody Guthrie's legacy
item.description: In the series "USA to Z," which celebrates 250 years of American history and culture, Anthony Mason talks to singer-songwriter Jesse Welles about Woody Guthrie's legacy and the meaning behind the popular song "This Land is Your Land."

item.title: CBS to host "The Great American Block Party 250" special on July 4
item.description: CBS will host a primetime special celebrating America's 250th birthday on Saturday, July 4, with exclusive performances and the largest fireworks show in history.

item.title: America's Block Party to celebrate July 4th with benefit concert
item.description: America's Block Party will celebrate the nation's 250th birthday this Fourth of July with a benefit concert in Los Angeles and events in local communities around the country.

item.title: "Do not open until July 4, 2276": What will America's Time Capsule reveal?
item.description: To mark America's 250th, a time capsule will be buried in Philadelphia on July 4, not to be opened until America's quincentennial. What objects made the cut to be preserved for another 250 years?

item.title: History and inspiration behind Uncle Sam
item.description: In the series "USA to Z," "CBS Mornings" breaks down the history of Uncle Sam and who inspired the national icon.

item.title: What are Freedom 250 and America250? Behind the 2 groups planning celebrations
item.description: A musical concert series has become a point of political contention, with performers dropping out of the series.

item.title: Unveiling the history beneath the Lincoln Memorial
item.description: Beneath the Lincoln Memorial is one of Washington's best-kept secrets: the Undercroft, a soaring 50,000-square-foot foundation built to keep the landmark from sinking into D.C.'s swampy ground. Now home to a museum, the public is being invited to visit underground.

item.title: Behind the rise of fast food and how it shaped the U.S.
item.description: In the series "USA to Z," "CBS Mornings" goes behind the scenes for a taste of history and shows how fast food has become part of American culture.

item.title: One man's determination to preserve a small Ohio town's civil rights history
item.description: David Butcher's family settled in Tablertown, Ohio in 1830. He spoke with "CBS Saturday Morning" about his goal to preserve local civil rights legacy with the Tablertown People of Color Museum.

item.title: Behind the controversial legacy of Mount Rushmore
item.description: In the series "USA to Z," "CBS Mornings" looks at the history and controversial legacy of Mount Rushmore, which depicts four U.S. presidents. Vladimir Duthiers reports.

item.title: These United States: An ode to baseball
item.description: If our national pastime has a national anthem, it is "Take Me Out to the Ball Game," penned in 1908 as a celebration of the sport. Correspondent Lee Cowan looks at the history of Jack Norworth and Albert Von Tilzer's song, which expresses the thrills, fun and romance of baseball.

item.title: Amanda Gorman on what it means to be an American | 60 Minutes
item.description: American poet Amanda Gorman says it's "more than just remembering our history. It's how we heal it. It's how we recover. It's how we hold ourselves accountable to being the best that we can be."

item.title: Inside the history, evolution and future of Hollywood
item.description: Hollywood is known for its iconic sign, Walk of Fame and studio lots bringing movies to life – but in 2026 the future of the entertainment industry is changing. Jo Ling Kent reports.

item.title: Norah O'Donnell on "We the Women," about the unsung heroines of America
item.description: The CBS News journalist's new book tells the often-overlooked stories of women who helped shape our nation, from the single female whose name appears on the Declaration of Independence, to the first Black woman to argue a case before the Supreme Court.

item.title: Ken Burns on what he thinks would surprise the founders of America | 60 Minutes
item.description: Filmmaker Ken Burns, who's helped viewers understand American history, shared what he thinks would surprise the nation's founders 250 years after the Declaration of Independence.

item.title: Community preserves history and reclaims once segregated bowling alley
item.description: The owner of a bowling alley in a small South Carolina city refused to integrate in the 1960s. It spurred peaceful protests until tensions erupted into what's now known as the "Orangeburg Massacre."

item.title: Trump announces IndyCar race will come to D.C. streets for America's 250th
item.description: President Trump announced that an IndyCar race, the Freedom 250 Grand Prix, will be held through the streets of Washington, D.C., as part of America's 250th birthday celebrations.

item.title: America at 250 – and a reckoning for President Trump
item.description: In this landmark year for American democracy, historian Lindsay Chervinsky, Washington Post columnist George F. Will, and Atlantic staff writer Vivian Salama talk about what the second year of Trump's presidency may mean for America's future.

item.title: These United States: Raising the flag
item.description: For decades, John Monsky's passion has been collecting American flags – from those that accompanied soldiers during the Civil War and on the beaches of France on D-Day, to ones that landed on the Moon. He tells Mo Rocca that the flags in his collection have been both witnesses to and participants in history.

item.title: These United States: Ken Burns on America's continuing revolution
item.description: Documentary filmmaker Ken Burns has long been a chronicler of the American experience. He talks with John Dickerson about his latest film for PBS, "The American Revolution"; the importance of studying history; and why the end of the Revolutionary War did not mean the end of our nation's revolution.

item.title: These United States: The White House
item.description: Since our second president, John Adams, inhabited the White House, it has been burned by British troops, reconstructed, expanded, and gut-renovated. Mo Rocca looks at the history of a cherished American landmark, which this past week was partly demolished by the White House's current occupant, President Trump, who is building a huge, privately-funded ballroom.

item.title: A history of America's Constitution
item.description: The checks-and-balances of our government have been increasingly tested in ways our founding fathers never anticipated, yet the Constitution remains, in the words of one expert, "absolutely eternal and timeless."

item.title: These United States: A history of the Constitution
item.description: Over the past several decades, the checks-and-balances of our government have been increasingly tested in ways our founding fathers never anticipated. Tony Dokoupil talks with Jill Lepore, author of "We the People: A History of the U.S. Constitution," and Jeffrey Rosen, president and CEO of the National Constitution Center, about our nation's bedrock document, which Rosen calls "absolutely eternal and timeless."

item.title: Ken Burns on how the American Revolution "turned the world upside down"
item.description: Filmmaker Ken Burns says the American Revolution created a "new thing called a citizen."
